label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what conclusion we can draw from the author's parents' story. The options are A to D. First, I need to recall the story. The parents had a kidney transplant where the mom donated a kidney to the dad. Before this, they had many quarrels, but after the surgery, their relationship improved. The dad became gentler and more patient, the mom became less angry, and there was more closeness. The family also mentions that the transplant healed the whole family, and they now have deeper faith. The author gave them photos of their hands clasped on the wedding day and during the recovery, linking the vows "for better or worse" with the recovery experience. The key points here are that before the surgery (the suffering), there were quarrels, and after the surgery, their relationship improved. So the suffering (the illness and surgery) led to a better relationship. Looking at the options: A) Illness is part of daily life. That's maybe true, but the story isn't really about illness being a normal part of life. It's more about how the illness (and the transplant) affected their relationship. B) Quarrels can't be avoided between parents. The story says that the parents had many quarrels before, but that after the surgery, the relationship improved. So the quarrels weren't unavoidable, they just changed after the event. So B might not be correct. C) The relationship between parents affects children greatly. The author does mention being affected by their parents' relationship, but the story's main point isn't about how the children are affected. It's more about how the parents' relationship improved through the suffering. So maybe C is not the main conclusion here. D) Suffering can better people's relationship as well. This seems to
